title: Check yourself: forms
questions.0.q: There is a field and a button. They press the button. Nothing happens. What is missing?
questions.0.options.0: JavaScript — forms do not work without it
questions.0.options.1: A <form> with an action and a method. A few fields and a button are not a form
questions.0.hint: The button presses. But towards what? Who tells it where everything typed should go?
questions.0.explain: What is missing is the tag that binds them and says WHERE (action) and HOW (method) it all goes. Without it the button presses into thin air.
questions.1.q: The form submits, but one field never arrives. The rest do. Why?
questions.1.options.0: It has no name. Data travels as name-value pairs; with no name the value has no address
questions.1.hint: The form does submit — so the form is not the problem. Compare this field with the others. What do they have that it does not?
questions.1.explain: A field with no name is a field that does not exist. Like posting a letter without writing who it is for — the server discards it.
questions.2.q: A login form with a password. Which method?
questions.2.options.1: POST — GET puts the password in the address bar, the history and the server logs
questions.2.options.3: Neither — passwords are not sent with a form
questions.2.hint: One of the two puts what was typed on screen, in the address bar. Ask yourself where a password must NOT end up.
questions.2.explain: GET is for READING (search, filter) — the address becomes shareable. POST is for CHANGING (login, order). A password with GET sits on screen for anyone behind you to read.
questions.3.q:
Above the field it says "Email:" in a

. It looks perfect. What is the problem?
questions.3.options.0: No problem — the label is above the field
questions.3.options.1: There is no link between the text and the field. A reader says only "text field" and the person cannot tell what to type
questions.3.options.2:
It should be an

, not a
questions.3.hint: It looks perfect — on screen. Ask what the computer sees: does it know these two lines have anything to do with each other?
questions.3.explain: Proximity is not a connection — it is a coincidence on screen. <label for="email"> + <input id="email"> makes the real link. Bonus: click the label and the cursor jumps into the field.
questions.4.q: Two radio buttons: Cash and Card. A person manages to select BOTH. Why?
questions.4.options.0: They have different names. A shared name is what makes them a group — it says "we are one question"
questions.4.hint: Radio buttons rule each other out only if the computer knows they are ONE question. What tells it that — the fact that they sit next to each other in the code?
questions.4.explain: The group is made by the name, not by proximity in the code. Different names → two separate questions, each with one answer. That is why both can be on.
questions.5.q: They build a button as a <div> with CSS and JavaScript. It works. What do they lose?
questions.5.options.0: Nothing — it looks and works like a button
questions.5.options.1: Tab cannot reach it, Enter does not press it, a reader never announces it as a button, and it does not submit the form
questions.5.options.3: That it cannot have a colour
questions.5.hint: It works — with a mouse. Put the mouse away and try again. How far do you get?
questions.5.explain: A <div> is a generic element — it has no role. It takes no focus, so Tab never finds it. And it can never be announced as a button: someone jumping from button to button does not even know it exists. You write twenty lines to rebuild something you were given for free — and it still comes out worse. Press and it ACTS → <button>. Press and it GOES somewhere → <a>.
questions.6.q: They sell three models. How should they ask which one the person wants?
questions.6.options.0: A text field — let the person type the model
questions.6.options.1: A <select> or radio buttons. When the answers are a fixed set, do not ask an open question
questions.6.hint: There are exactly three answers and you know them in advance. Why make the person type them?
questions.6.explain: An open field will receive "the black one", "black bike", "the one below" and three kinds of typo. Then you sit there guessing. Offer a choice.
questions.7.q: They added required and type="email". Does that mean the data reaching the server is validated?
questions.7.options.0: Yes — the browser will not let bad data through
questions.7.options.1: No. Those checks are for convenience. Anyone can switch them off from the browser tools in ten seconds
questions.7.options.2: Yes, if there is a placeholder too
questions.7.hint: Ask WHERE those checks run. On whose computer? And who is in charge of that computer?
questions.7.explain: Browser validation helps an honest person avoid mistakes. It does not stop a dishonest one. The real check happens on the server, always.
